text: We report the case of a sixty-four years old male that came to our attention due to a four-centimeter tumor of the right colon and two synchronous liver metastases in the right lobe. Moreover, the preoperative imaging showed a six-centimeter tumor suspect for HCC in segment six. Therefore, after multidisciplinary evaluation, the patient was scheduled for upfront right hypotectomy and right colectomy with robotic approach.

The surgical team prepares for an extracorporeal Pringle maneuver by placing a tape around the hepatic hilum, which is tied using a chest tube passed through a 12mm trocar. The procedure begins with dissection and isolation of the right hepatic artery, which is suspended on a tape. The right portal vein is similarly isolated and suspended, and both the right portal vein and artery are temporarily clamped with a bulldog clamp to identify the cantilever line.

The surgeon performs intraoperative ultrasound to identify the liver lesion and marks the transection line with an adequate margin. Following this, the bulldog clamps are released, and both the right hepatic artery and portal vein are divided using Hem-o-lok clips.

The surgeon isolates and controls accessory hepatic veins using clips or a stapler, depending on their size. The right lobe is fully immobilized, and the right hepatic vein is isolated, encircled, and divided using a vascular stapler.

The surgeon divides the liver parenchyma using the Kelly crash technique with bipolar Maryland forceps and a harmonic scalpel. The right biliary duct is carefully isolated within the liver tissue and divided using Hemlock clips for safety.

The surgeon divides another branch of the right hepatic vein, completing the right hepatectomy. Attention then shifts to the right colon, where the ileocolic vessels are ligated. The last ileal loop and the transverse colon are stapled, and the specimen is collected in a laparoscopic bag.
The surgeon prepares for a manual intracorporeal anastomosis using a running 3-0 V-Loc suture, indicating the start of the suturing process within the surgical field.
The final surgical specimen is displayed, and the abdomen is shown at the conclusion of the procedure.
